segments.title: The industrial price of a Soviet treaty fleet
segments.summary: A Soviet battleship program would require earlier investment in armor plate, heavy industry, and imported plant from established foreign suppliers.
segments.body: The counterfactual begins with industrial capacity rather than a list of ships. Soviet five-year plans could prioritize heavy industry, but capital-ship armor demanded specialized plant and expertise beyond ordinary steel output. Dr. Clarke identifies Vickers and Bethlehem Steel as plausible sources for the machinery and know-how needed to establish domestic armor production.

evidence.note: The transcript connects an earlier Soviet naval buildup to five-year-plan priorities, armor production, and imported industrial equipment.

segments.title: Why Stalin might enter the London Naval Conference
segments.summary: German participation, prestige, and security provide a narrow route for Stalin to send a delegation and demand a four-fleet capital-ship program.
segments.body: The plausible divergence is not an urgent Soviet naval need but Stalin's reaction to the possibility that Germany might be invited. A delegation led by naval and diplomatic figures could then press for 35,000-ton, 16-inch-gun ships along with cruisers, destroyers, and submarines. Those demands reflect the Soviet requirement to support Baltic, Black Sea, Northern, and Pacific fleets while preserving the regime's image of strength.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the German-invitation trigger, likely Soviet delegates, and Stalin's minimum fleet ambitions.

segments.title: The London treaty's existing fault lines
segments.summary: British cruiser coverage, Japanese exit planning, and Franco-Italian rivalry already made the 1930 settlement fragile before adding Soviet demands.
segments.body: Britain wanted enough light cruisers for worldwide presence, while cumulative tonnage limits threatened to deepen the Royal Navy's cruiser gap. Japan accepted restrictions while preparing for greater freedom after 1936, and France resisted limits that would leave it unable to balance Italy in the Mediterranean and protect global commitments. The Soviet delegation therefore enters negotiations already crowded with incompatible strategic requirements.

evidence.note: The transcript surveys British cruiser needs, Japanese planning, and the linked French and Italian positions.

segments.title: A six-power treaty tonnage bargain
segments.summary: Fitting the Soviet Union into the treaty raises capital-ship, carrier, cruiser, and destroyer allowances while making submarines Moscow's principal concession.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke proposes twelve battleships for both Japan and the Soviet Union, which pushes Britain and the United States toward twenty each under the established ratio logic. Carrier tonnage and cruiser allowances also rise because the leading navies must account for another major fleet. Soviet submarine tonnage is capped far below Stalin's aspirational numbers, but parity with the other leading powers could still be presented domestically as a diplomatic victory.

evidence.note: The transcript works through proposed capital-ship, carrier, cruiser, destroyer, and submarine allocations.

segments.title: How many Soviet capital ships could actually be built
segments.summary: Yard capacity and long construction times limit the Soviet payoff, with perhaps six early ships nearing service and extensive foreign technology inside Soviet hulls.
segments.body: Only a few Baltic and Black Sea yards initially possess the physical space for ships of this size, while Northern and Pacific facilities would need major expansion. Even with an early start, the estimate is that no more than six of the first eight ships would be close to service by 1939. Their hulls, armor, and guns could be Soviet-built, but propulsion, gearing, and fire control would likely depend on licensed or imported Italian and other foreign systems.

evidence.note: The transcript estimates Soviet yard capacity, completion times, fleet priorities, and reliance on foreign equipment.

segments.title: British and American battleship and carrier responses
segments.summary: Earlier North Carolina-type battleships and larger carriers become plausible once treaty allocations expand to counter an additional major fleet.
segments.body: The United States could move earlier toward the North Carolina design pattern, while Britain would face a political and technical choice between 15-inch and 16-inch guns on fast 35,000-ton ships. Higher carrier tonnage reduces the pressure to trade air-group size against protection, making larger British and American fleet carriers more practical. The result could be Ark Royal- or Yorktown-derived ships with stronger flight decks and greater survivability before the historical rearmament cycle.

evidence.note: The transcript outlines earlier US battleships, British gun and layout choices, and less constrained fleet-carrier designs.

segments.title: French, Italian, and Japanese construction choices
segments.summary: France could build enlarged Suffren-derived fast ships, Italy accelerate Littorio development, and Japan use treaty battleships to prepare its yards for Yamato-scale construction.
segments.body: France's enlarged Suffren concept offers a fast alternative to the historical Dunkerque path, while Italian export income and industrial activity could accelerate new battleships beyond the reconstructed older fleet. Japan gains three modern 16-inch-gun ships and, just as importantly, a funded route to expand the infrastructure later needed for much larger vessels. That stronger Japanese program could alter both naval aviation growth and the balance surrounding the 1939 Soviet-Japanese conflict.

evidence.note: The transcript considers French and Italian fast battleships, Japanese treaty construction, and infrastructure for later expansion.

segments.title: Germany faces an earlier Allied naval rearmament
segments.summary: Earlier British, American, French, and Soviet construction could make Germany's balanced surface fleet unaffordable and its commerce raiders far easier to contain.
segments.body: Germany begins from the industrial restrictions imposed after the First World War and cannot match fleets whose shipyards have already restarted large-warship production. Diverting more resources to the navy would weaken the army and air force, while a submarine-centered alternative would provoke earlier escort construction. More fast battleships and carriers also shrink the operating space for Deutschland- and Scharnhorst-type raiders, changing the strategic value of Germany's historical naval buildup.

evidence.note: The transcript compares German industrial limits with earlier foreign rearmament and explains the effect on raiders and fleet strategy.
